segments.title: Why littoral danger is not a new naval lesson
segments.summary: Ukraine's uncrewed craft update a long-standing warning about operating close to a defended coast rather than creating an entirely new prohibition.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke challenges claims that Ukraine newly proved ships cannot operate within 100 miles of land. Mines, aircraft, coastal artillery, submarines, torpedo boats, and explosive craft have made coastlines dangerous for more than a century; the Falklands exclusion zone already reflected that scale of risk. The practical lesson is to operate farther out when possible and to enter the inner littoral with a properly supported task force rather than a lone ship.

evidence.note: The discussion compares modern uncrewed threats with older coastal weapons and explains the 100- and 200-mile operating rules.

segments.title: Amphibious warfare survives through force and geography
segments.summary: Successful landings avoid an enemy's strongest point, suppress defenses, and create or bring the infrastructure needed to sustain the force ashore.
segments.body: The survival of mines, missiles, aircraft, and artillery does not make amphibious operations impossible, but it makes casual or under-supported assaults untenable. Normandy illustrates the preference for landing away from the strongest defended port and supplying the lodgement through purpose-built infrastructure. The recurring requirement is enough firepower, planning, and logistics to exploit a weaker section of coast.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses defended ports, Normandy, Mulberry harbours, mines, and the support needed for a landing.

segments.title: Information and layered defense remain decisive
segments.summary: Early information, overlapping defenses, redundancy, and acceptance of losses matter more than promises that one advanced system will make war clean.
segments.body: Drones fit an old pattern: reconnaissance makes artillery and other weapons more effective by finding targets sooner. Dr. Clarke links that information advantage to layered defense and argues that generations repeatedly forget it when promised a cheaper, more efficient single system. War remains adversarial and messy, so modern ships can still be sunk and even a sound plan can fail because the enemy acts intelligently too.

evidence.note: The speaker connects reconnaissance, layered defenses, repeated institutional forgetting, and the continuing vulnerability of ships.

segments.title: The Falklands as an expeditionary logistics war
segments.summary: Britain's 8,000-mile deployment worked through auxiliary shipping and improvised sustainment, while Argentina struggled to coordinate and sustain forces closer to home.
segments.body: Geography imposed expeditionary demands on both combatants, though in different ways. Britain relied on the Royal Fleet Auxiliary, ships taken up from trade, Ascension Island, and extensive re-stowage to sustain a distant task force. Argentina possessed the shorter route but lacked the infrastructure and coordination needed to turn proximity into a reliable operational advantage.

evidence.note: The lecture contrasts British auxiliary logistics with Argentina's limited ability to sustain and coordinate its nearby forces.

segments.title: How San Carlos Bay turned geography into defense
segments.summary: Michael Clapp's force selected San Carlos Bay to obstruct missile approaches, avoid the strongest defense, and establish a lodgement for the advance on Stanley.
segments.body: The amphibious command combined local knowledge with the perspective of an officer trained to attack ships from aircraft. Hills and rock around San Carlos denied easy stand-off missile shots and forced attackers into more exposed bombing runs. Instead of assaulting Port Stanley directly, the British landed where defenses were weaker, built a base, and advanced with naval gunfire and artillery support after the loss of Atlantic Conveyor reduced helicopter mobility.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Clapp's planning criteria, local knowledge, Argentina's expectations, and the San Carlos landing scheme.

segments.title: The cost of missing carrier airborne early warning
segments.summary: Reliance on allied land-based coverage left the Falklands task force without organic airborne early warning when it fought far outside its planned NATO setting.
segments.body: British plans assumed Norwegian, British, or American aircraft would supply early warning during the expected NATO mission. The Falklands deployment exposed the danger of specializing away a capability needed for independent operations. Helicopter AEW appeared quickly after the war, but too late to give the task force the warning time that might have reduced ship losses and protected Atlantic Conveyor.

evidence.note: The speaker explains why organic AEW was omitted and estimates the operational effect of having it in 1982.

segments.title: Why naval guns complete the defensive layers
segments.summary: The Falklands demonstrated that missiles alone do not replace guns for close defense, warning shots, shore support, and suppression of predictable attack routes.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke contrasts precision missile defenses with the volume of fire needed when aircraft or missiles are already close. The gun-poor configuration of some British ships reduced both fire support and the last defensive layers, while the Italian Navy's heavily armed ships reflect Mediterranean expectations of short warning times. In San Carlos, commanders wanted weapons capable of saturating likely approach corridors, not merely another highly precise single shot.

evidence.note: The discussion covers gunless Type 22s, Italian armament, distance-time-force, and Clapp's desire for volume fire at San Carlos.

segments.title: What the Black Buck runway attack achieved
segments.question: Did Britain bomb the Falklands runway from Ascension Island, and what effect did it have?
segments.answerShort: A Vulcan raid damaged the runway and discouraged jet operations, although C-130 flights continued and the raid's exact material effect remains debated.
segments.summary: A concise assessment separates the debated physical damage from the runway attack's operational and signaling effects.
segments.body: The Vulcan attack did not completely close the runway. It helped prevent the use of larger jet aircraft, while nighttime Hercules flights continued; those flights could also reveal useful intelligence to the British. The answer therefore treats Black Buck as consequential without claiming it solved the air problem by itself.

evidence.note: The audience asks about bombing the runway and the response discusses Vulcan damage and continuing C-130 flights.

segments.title: What the Falklands exclusion zone was for
segments.question: Was the exclusion zone a free-fire zone or a defined combat area?
segments.answerShort: It served operational, legal, political, and civilian-safety purposes while warning Argentine forces away and offering room to de-escalate.
segments.summary: The answer explains why the exclusion zone was more than a simple permission to fire at anything inside it.
segments.body: The zone marked the expected combat area, discouraged civilian traffic, and clarified the warning given to neutral shipping. It also offered Argentina a way to keep its fleet away from the fight and allowed Britain to frame action against approaching warships as defensive enforcement. Its meaning combined military practice with law and political signaling.

evidence.note: The prompt asks the zone's status and the answer lays out its combat, civilian, legal, and de-escalatory roles.

segments.title: How Exocet changed the threat model
segments.question: Did the Falklands War cause a shift toward anti-missile defense in the Royal Navy and NATO?
segments.answerShort: The shift had begun, but Exocet exposed how heavily defenses had been optimized for Soviet-style attacks rather than a Western sea-skimming missile profile.
segments.summary: Exocet's importance lay partly in forcing navies to broaden the missile characteristics against which their defenses were designed.
segments.body: NATO was already developing missile defenses, including systems such as Sea Wolf. The war showed that preparing mainly for Soviet weapon profiles could leave gaps against a French-designed missile with different operational characteristics. Subsequent Western work therefore had a relevant foundation for later threats such as Kalibr.

evidence.note: The answer contrasts Soviet missile assumptions with Exocet's characteristics and later Western defensive work.

segments.title: Would Ark Royal have ended the war in two weeks?
segments.question: Would Ark Royal, Phantoms, and airborne early warning have won the Falklands War within a fortnight?
segments.answerShort: They would likely have won the air battle faster and reduced losses, but troops still had to land, defeat the garrison, and complete the ground campaign.
segments.summary: More carrier air power could accelerate the campaign without removing its amphibious and land-war requirements.
segments.body: The answer rejects a purely air-centric reading of the war. Better fighters and AEW would have protected shipping and may have preserved Atlantic Conveyor, improving mobility ashore. Yet the Argentine garrison still required defeat, and the answer also clarifies the separate carrier, amphibious, land, and overall task-force command roles.

evidence.note: The prompt cites the fortnight claim and the response distinguishes air superiority from completing the land campaign.

segments.title: Why Fifth Brigade struggled with amphibious logistics
segments.question: Did helicopter losses force the Navy to share scarce amphibious resources with the Army?
segments.answerShort: The deeper problem was that Fifth Brigade had little relevant joint or expeditionary training and did not understand the tight sequencing of amphibious resources.
segments.summary: Training and organizational preparation mattered as much as the physical loss of helicopters and landing assets.
segments.body: Army formations accustomed to a continental NATO setting could expect equipment to be borrowed or shifted locally. In an amphibious operation, each craft and item often has a scheduled role in a constrained ship-to-shore sequence. Fifth Brigade had barely trained together for this mission, leaving its staff poorly prepared for those limits.

evidence.note: The response explains the contrasting Army and naval logistics assumptions and Fifth Brigade's lack of preparation.

segments.title: Layered defense instead of one perfect weapon
segments.summary: A warship's defense comes from complementary sensors and weapons across several ranges, not from demanding that one gun defeat every threat.
segments.body: Different systems perform different jobs, and their combined capabilities make the ship useful; ships then reinforce one another as a formation. Against a fast missile, fighters and area-defense weapons should engage first, point-defense missiles follow, and close-in guns remain the final layer. Dr. Clarke argues that 20 mm fire may lack the energy needed against some modern missiles, but that limitation does not invalidate the layered concept.

evidence.note: The speaker answers linked questions by describing ships as collections of systems and tracing the successive defensive layers.

segments.title: Submarine and aviation lessons from 1982
segments.question: What lessons did submarine and naval aviation communities draw from the Falklands War?
segments.answerShort: Aviation urgently needed organic early warning, while British submariners needed dependable torpedoes; a Second World War weapon sank Belgrano amid problems with newer types.
segments.summary: The short answer points to two concrete capability gaps: warning aircraft and reliable torpedoes.
segments.body: The aviation lesson returns to the absence of organic AEW over the task force. For submarines, weapon reliability was central: the successful attack on General Belgrano used older torpedoes, while problems with modern weapons shaped other engagements. The exchange is a reminder that a platform's theoretical power depends on working sensors and ordnance.

evidence.note: The question explicitly asks for submarine and aviation lessons and the answer identifies AEW and torpedo reliability.

segments.title: Ukraine separates amphibious assault from coastal raiding
segments.summary: Russia lacked the concentrated fleet and trained naval infantry needed for a major landing, while Ukraine still demonstrated that smaller amphibious raids remain viable.
segments.body: Russian planning assumed a rapid collapse and did not assemble enough naval strength to suppress a defended coast. Once Ukraine survived the opening and fortified likely landing areas, assaulting an urbanized shoreline became exceptionally costly. Yet Ukrainian small-unit raids show that amphibious activity has not disappeared; the distinction is between limited raiding and a sustained assault able to land, supply, and withdraw a large force.

evidence.note: The lecture covers Russian underestimation, absent fleet concentration, naval-infantry priorities, and Ukrainian coastal raids.

segments.title: Moskva and the danger of solo patrols
segments.summary: Moskva's isolated deployment revealed both contempt for the opponent and a fleet too small to provide mutual support, rotation, and redundancy.
segments.body: Even a fully upgraded cruiser should not patrol a high-threat area alone. A second hull adds sensors, weapons, rescue capacity, tactical choices, and the ability to rotate ships through maintenance and rest. Dr. Clarke compares Moskva with historical British deployments in which even a powerful cruiser received a smaller companion because commanders expected uncertainty and resistance.

evidence.note: The lecture uses Moskva and a 1939 British cruiser-sloop deployment to explain why a second hull creates options.

segments.title: Why Starlink is difficult to jam
segments.question: Why has Russia struggled to jam Starlink effectively?
segments.answerShort: Directional satellite links and the very large constellation make broad, persistent jamming or spoofing expensive and difficult.
segments.summary: The answer explains how link geometry and constellation scale complicate electronic attack on satellite communications.
segments.body: A satellite terminal communicates upward rather than broadcasting laterally across a wide terrestrial area. An attacker may overload or spoof particular links or satellites, but Starlink's numbers create a difficult scaling problem. Destroying satellites would carry still larger technical and political consequences.

evidence.note: The prompt asks about Starlink jamming and the answer describes link directionality and the constellation's scale.

segments.title: Drones as reconnaissance and artillery enablers
segments.summary: Ukraine's drones chiefly compress the sensor-to-shooter cycle, letting observers find targets while artillery supplies scalable firepower.
segments.body: Satellite links, commercial imagery, and international technical exchange help Ukraine exploit its wider information environment. Small drones keep human observers under cover, locate targets, correct fire, and sometimes strike directly, but they do not abolish artillery. Both sides are adapting within a high-technology war of attrition in which immature systems evolve quickly and information speed can offset disadvantages in mass.

evidence.note: The discussion links Starlink, commercial information, drone reconnaissance, artillery observation, and faster operational reaction.

segments.title: Raw footage and the wider information war
segments.summary: Ukraine's rapid release of comparatively raw footage helped frame public understanding, while battlefield evidence also exposed the gap between sales claims and real weapon performance.
segments.body: Operational information shapes external political support as well as battlefield decisions. By supplying more immediate visual evidence, Ukraine influenced debate among both friendly and hostile audiences. The same evidence warns against reading brochure specifications as normal battlefield performance, because advertised ranges and reliability often depend on ideal maintenance and environmental conditions.

evidence.note: The lecture discusses Ukraine's footage, political effects, and the limitations of defense-sales specifications.

segments.title: HMS Argus and the older history of drone carriers
segments.summary: Claims for the first drone carrier overlook HMS Argus's pre-war operation of Queen Bee radio-controlled target aircraft.
segments.body: The Royal Navy used HMS Argus with Queen Bee target aircraft in 1939, and the terminology around the Queen Bee helped popularize the word 'drone.' These aircraft supported anti-aircraft training and were close to being weaponized, though contemporary radio control was considered too vulnerable to jamming. Modern uncrewed aviation is therefore an acceleration of a much older line of experimentation.

evidence.note: The speaker identifies Argus, Queen Bee target aircraft, the drone terminology, and the jamming concern.

segments.title: How Ukraine's geography sustains a long war
segments.summary: Defensible terrain, exposed landing areas, national identity, and competing political stakes make the conflict resistant to a quick military or diplomatic solution.
segments.body: The coastline offers few attractive amphibious options: an Odessa landing would enter a narrow, defended area exposed to artillery and missiles. Inland terrain supplies natural defensive lines, while Ukraine's proximity to its industry and improving European transport links supports continued resistance. Human geography matters too, because the war has become central to Ukrainian identity and to the Russian political system's claim to success, leaving both sides with strong reasons not to concede.

evidence.note: The lecture examines the coastline, Odessa, defensive depth, national identity, infrastructure, and Moskva's exposed position.

segments.title: Technology complicates war without changing its fundamentals
segments.summary: Across the world wars, the Falklands, and Ukraine, new systems alter execution but repeatedly confirm the need for information, mass, redundancy, logistics, and layered firepower.
segments.body: Institutions often forget old requirements because doing so makes peacetime budgets easier, then relearn them after combat. Dr. Clarke uses a heavily gunned Type 31 concept to illustrate redundancy at close range: several mounts provide overlapping protection and allow the ship to keep fighting after a failure. The broader conclusion is not that every proposed fit is mandatory, but that technology cannot eliminate adversarial uncertainty or the need for depth.

evidence.note: The summary compares successive wars and uses a multi-gun frigate design to demonstrate redundancy and layered protection.

segments.title: How the Falklands restored British military prestige
segments.question: How much did victory improve Britain's standing through the rest of the 1980s and into the Gulf War?
segments.answerShort: The campaign demonstrated resolve and expeditionary competence, sustaining prestige until later force cuts—especially the 2010 carrier gap—undermined it.
segments.summary: The Falklands mattered not only as a battlefield victory but as evidence that Britain retained the will and means to act at distance.
segments.body: The answer treats the war as a foundation for later confidence in British military capability. It also argues that public support for the islanders left any government little room to accept conquest by a dictatorship without fighting. Later removal of Harriers and the carrier capability is presented as a sharper break with that reputation than the immediate postwar period.

evidence.note: The audience asks about prestige and the answer connects the victory, political resolve, and the later carrier gap.

segments.title: Why Russia's opening plan had a narrow window
segments.question: Did Russia repeat the Dardanelles pattern by telegraphing its attack?
segments.answerShort: Yes; Russia needed a rapid seizure of Kyiv before Ukraine organized, but failure in the opening hours allowed outside supplies and political resistance to turn the campaign into a long war.
segments.summary: The answer links operational surprise, logistics, alliance support, and political messaging in the failure of the 2022 opening gamble.
segments.body: The initial airport and Kyiv operations offered a chance only if Ukrainian command collapsed almost immediately. Once resistance held and NATO partners began supplying equipment, the time horizon expanded from days toward years. President Zelenskyy's decision to remain in Kyiv and his public messaging helped mobilize the political support required to sustain that flow.

evidence.note: The response compares telegraphed attacks, the opening Kyiv operation, allied supplies, and Zelenskyy's information campaign.

segments.title: Could Ukraine join NATO after the war?
segments.question: Could Ukraine eventually be allowed to join NATO?
segments.answerShort: Membership would require peace with Russia and unanimous allied consent, while a close but formally non-member relationship is another plausible outcome.
segments.summary: The answer outlines the institutional and diplomatic conditions behind any future Ukrainian NATO settlement.
segments.body: Formal accession cannot be separated from the unresolved war and NATO's unanimity rule. Dr. Clarke also sketches an alternative in which Ukraine becomes deeply aligned and interoperable with NATO while remaining legally outside the alliance. The comparison is to Cold War states that were strategically close to one bloc yet retained a distinct formal position.

evidence.note: The prompt asks about NATO membership and the answer covers peace, unanimity, and a close non-member model.

segments.title: The Moskva lesson navies may choose to avoid
segments.question: Will Moskva's loss teach other navies lasting lessons?
segments.answerShort: It should, but navies may blame poor maintenance and upgrades because acknowledging the need for more ships and mutual support costs much more.
segments.summary: Institutional learning can stop at the cheapest explanation even when a loss also exposes inadequate fleet numbers and deployment doctrine.
segments.body: Moskva's condition plainly mattered, but it is not the whole explanation. A single unsupported ship was asked to remain continuously on station in a threat area. Concentrating only on defects aboard the cruiser allows institutions to avoid the more expensive lesson about escorts, rotations, and fleet size.

evidence.note: The question asks whether navies will learn and the answer contrasts maintenance explanations with the cost of force-structure reform.

segments.title: Precision versus volume in close defense
segments.question: How useful is programmable 40 mm 3P ammunition for modern naval defense?
segments.answerShort: It is valuable on Type 31, but programmable rounds should be paired with enough barrels and rate of fire to saturate a last-ditch threat zone.
segments.summary: Advanced ammunition improves lethality, yet the final defensive layer may still need volume more than perfect single-shot accuracy.
segments.body: Programmable, pre-fragmented, proximity-fused ammunition gives 40 mm and 57 mm guns flexible effects against varied targets. Dr. Clarke nevertheless favors twin mounts because a weapon facing an imminent missile must destroy it immediately and tolerate small aiming errors. The tradeoff is between precision engineering and the density of fire needed when failure means a mission kill or loss of the ship.

evidence.note: The response evaluates 3P ammunition, Type 31 mounts, and the accuracy-versus-rate-of-fire tradeoff.

segments.title: Will explosive USVs become the new torpedo boats?
segments.question: Will most navies acquire small explosive USVs as modern equivalents of torpedo boats?
segments.answerShort: Many will develop designs and related mine-warfare craft, but peacetime fleets may defer buying expendable attack versions when conventional torpedoes already cover part of the role.
segments.summary: Wartime usefulness does not guarantee large peacetime inventories when existing weapons overlap and procurement budgets compete.
segments.body: Ukraine's requirements arise from its particular constraints and do not map directly onto every established navy. Other fleets may keep designs ready, experiment with uncrewed mine warfare, and wait to build explosive attack craft at scale until a crisis. The answer distinguishes technical possibility from a durable peacetime acquisition program.

evidence.note: The prompt compares explosive USVs to torpedo boats and the answer discusses designs, mine warfare, torpedoes, and peacetime procurement.

segments.title: Why fleet size improves availability nonlinearly
segments.question: Why were warship classes historically built in fours, and what does a group of four provide today?
segments.answerShort: Four once formed a division within an eight-ship squadron; today four hulls can guarantee one continuously available, while larger fleets make refit and training schedules more predictable and improve the deployable fraction.
segments.summary: The rule of three or four is a starting point, but larger classes gain additional resilience because maintenance and training can be distributed across more hulls.
segments.body: One ship may be in deep refit, another training or returning, and another deployed; a fourth protects continuous availability. Historical four-ship batches also supplied common divisions that combined into squadrons. As a class grows, schedulers gain flexibility, so twelve Type 45s could normally provide far more than twice the usable strength of the six actually built.

evidence.note: The answer traces historical divisions and squadrons, then explains refit, training, deployment, and scaling effects.

segments.title: How uncrewed boats support mine clearance
segments.question: Can modular uncrewed boats perform missions beyond carrying explosives?
segments.answerShort: Yes; mine clearance already uses uncrewed surface vessels, normally several working with a crewed support ship, though British procurement has left too few available forward.
segments.summary: Mine countermeasures show how uncrewed craft fit into a team rather than replacing the crewed mothership by themselves.
segments.body: A practical package may pair one mine-clearance vessel with three or four USVs. Discussion of a possible Strait of Hormuz mission exposed the gap between that concept and the small number of working systems actually deployed. The example reinforces the video's broader argument that useful technology still needs sufficient numbers, maintenance, and an operational formation.

evidence.note: The exchange describes UK mine-clearance USVs, typical team size, forward availability, and procurement limits.

segments.title: Why conventional deterrence deserves more study
segments.question: Does Dr. Clarke enjoy nuclear strategy games?
segments.answerShort: He has used them but focuses professionally on conventional deterrence because far fewer analysts study how ordinary forces prevent conflict and manage escalation.
segments.summary: The exchange distinguishes belief in nuclear deterrence from a research focus on the less crowded field of conventional deterrence.
segments.body: Nuclear deterrence has a large specialist literature and many exercises. Conventional deterrence asks how visible, usable military forces can prevent war without crossing the nuclear threshold, including the risks and force-management choices involved. The answer explains why that field is central to Dr. Clarke's work despite his familiarity with nuclear scenarios.

evidence.note: The prompt asks about nuclear games and the answer defines the speaker's conventional-deterrence specialization.

segments.title: The lost five-flight-deck force structure
segments.question: Should Britain have built a third Queen Elizabeth-class carrier?
segments.answerShort: Britain effectively paid far more than two hulls should have cost; an earlier concept of two strike carriers and three F-35B-capable LHDs would have provided five interchangeable flight decks and steadier availability.
segments.summary: A mixed carrier-and-LHD fleet could have matched Britain's global commitments with more resilient aviation and amphibious availability.
segments.body: The proposed structure combined two full strike carriers with three large-deck amphibious ships able to operate the same F-35B pool. At least two decks would usually be available, even when one or more ships were in maintenance, and the force would also retain well decks for amphibious operations. The answer emphasizes fleet-level interchangeability rather than treating a carrier as an isolated prestige platform.

evidence.note: The response describes the costs of two carriers and the two-carrier, three-LHD, common-F-35B concept.

segments.title: How fleet cuts damage recruiting and retention
segments.summary: A larger fleet can be easier to crew over time because it offers credible careers, promotion paths, and stable demand for trained personnel.
segments.body: Crewing levels follow the force a country chooses to build, so a third carrier would have required recruitment and retention planned years earlier. Repeated ship cuts remove billets and promotion opportunities, encouraging experienced people to leave because they no longer see a full career ahead. The two Queen Elizabeth-class carriers also replaced several older aviation and support ships, meaning headline hull counts conceal a broader contraction in career structure.

evidence.note: The discussion connects fleet scale with recruiting, promotion prospects, retention, and the five older ships replaced by two carriers.

segments.title: Why availability rules apply beyond ships
segments.question: Do the rules of three and four also apply to aircraft and tanks?
segments.answerShort: Broadly yes, because training, maintenance, and unpredictable operational demand affect all military equipment, though the exact model must match the asset.
segments.summary: Readiness ratios transfer across equipment categories, but commercial fleet software can mislead when military demand is less predictable and replacement assets cannot be rented quickly.
segments.body: The underlying issue is the cycle through operations, training, repair, and deep maintenance. Models developed for lorry fleets can understate military risk because commercial vehicles are easily hired and rarely face sudden crisis surges comparable to a carrier. Availability calculations must therefore reflect the actual platform and mission rather than merely reuse a convenient civilian model.

evidence.note: The answer generalizes readiness ratios and critiques carrier availability modeling derived from lorry management.

segments.title: Why Britain chose STOVL instead of catapults
segments.question: Why could Britain not design a carrier catapult?
segments.answerShort: Britain could design one, but the planned common fleet of carriers and slower LHDs favored STOVL aircraft that could operate from every deck; CATOBAR would have made more sense in a three-carrier force.
segments.summary: The absence of catapults followed the intended fleet architecture and common-aircraft concept rather than a simple lack of British technical ability.
segments.body: Large amphibious ships with well decks cannot generate carrier speeds, so a common F-35B force offered flexibility across five proposed flight decks. A fleet designed from the outset around three strike carriers could instead justify CATOBAR, its specialized air groups, and an indigenous or partnered catapult program. The French example also shows that a single CATOBAR carrier can depend heavily on allied training facilities despite claims of autonomy.

evidence.note: The response connects catapult choice with LHD speed, common STOVL aircraft, fleet size, and allied CATOBAR support.
